Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': * '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) * '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]][[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]]

The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': * '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) * '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]][[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Qq-1月4日 (旧暦)-qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

{{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': * '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) * '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]][[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq-1月4日 (旧暦)-qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': * '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) * '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]][[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': * '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) * '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]][[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

{{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': ** '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) ** '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]][[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': ** '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) ** '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}}[[Category:11]] {{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': ** '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) ** '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': ** '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) ** '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qq]][[ja:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': ** '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) ** '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qq]][[ja:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}}}} | location = [[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Nagareyama City]], [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]] 657 | coordinates = {{Coord|35|52|6.86|N|139|56|7.30|E|}} | deity = [[Takeminakata|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto]] | Status = Former [[Village Shrine]] | Main Hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] | Established = [[Daido (Japan)|Daido]] 2 (807) | Festival = [[August 23]] }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Komaki (Nagareyama)|Komaki]], [[Nagareyama]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''"Komaki no Osuwa-sama"''' or '''"Suwa-no-Miya"'''. Under the [[modern shrine Ranking system]], it was classified as a [[village shrine]]. The [[shrine crest]] is the "Myojin Kaji" (明神梶), the crest of [[Suwa Taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine’s Lower Shrine]]. == deity == * '''[[Takeminakata]] (健御名方富命)''' == History == The shrine is located on a plateau that serves as a watershed between the modern [[Tone River]] system (formerly part of the [[Kinu River]] before the [[Tone River diversion project]]) and the modern [[Edo River]] system (previously part of the [[Taito River]] and [[Watarase River]] before the project). Within its precincts lies the "Suwa Shrine Archaeological Site," which has revealed evidence of human habitation dating back to the late [[Jomon period]]. According to shrine legend, the shrine was founded in '''Daido 2 (807)''' when descendants of '''Prince Takatsu''', a son of [[Emperor Tenmu]], migrated to the area. They established this place as their permanent settlement and enshrined a '''branch of Suwa Grand Shrine''' from [[Shinano Province]] as their guardian deity. There is also a claim that an earlier shrine already existed in the reign of '''Emperor Keikō'''. This connection between Prince Takatsu and [[Miwa Takatsumaro]] suggests ties with [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] (worshiping [[Ōkuninushi]]) in [[Yamato Province]] and [[Munakata Taisha]] (worshiping [[Munakata Ōkami]]) in [[Chikuzen Province]]{{Cite web|title=Shrine Foundation|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3501.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. The shrine’s '''former betto-ji temple''' (guardian Buddhist temple) and '''Oku-no-in''' was '''Shōken-ji''' (成顕寺), said to have been founded during the Daido era by a disciple of '''Kūkai''' (Kōbō Daishi){{Cite web|title=Shōken-ji|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/8830|website=Chiba Prefecture Tourism Information|access-date=2019-08-10}}. [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. It is said that he procured warhorses here, and upon his victorious return, he dedicated a horse to the shrine in gratitude. The '''place name "Karakake" (鞍掛)''', located southeast of the shrine across [[Chiba Prefectural Route 278]], is said to originate from the legend that Yoshiie hung his saddle on a pine tree in the shrine grounds. Historically, the area surrounding Suwa Shrine was a horse-breeding region. Later, the '''Edo shogunate''' designated it as part of the '''Kogane Makiba''', a military horse ranch, making the shrine an important landmark for horse-related traditions. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. During the '''Edo period''', '''Tokugawa Mitsukuni''' (Mito Kōmon) is recorded as having visited the shrine. Since the '''Meiji era''', several nearby shrines have been relocated to Suwa Shrine’s precincts, resulting in many auxiliary shrines (摂末社). Even today, Suwa Shrine’s '''chief priest''' often conducts ceremonies at smaller local shrines without their own priests. The shrine serves as a central religious institution in the area, with a '''spacious forested precinct spanning about 10,000 tsubo (approximately 33,000 square meters)'''. == Grounds == [[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (02).jpg|thumb|230px|Entrance and First Torii Gate]] === Main Shrine Buildings === The original '''shrine hall''' was destroyed in a '''1749 (Kan’en 2) fire'''. The '''current main hall was rebuilt in 1826 (Bunsei 9)''', and the '''worship hall in 1846 (Kōka 3)'''{{Cite web|title=Shrine Buildings|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16242/dt3500.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}{{Cite web|title=Komaki Suwa Shrine (Chiba Prefecture)|url=http://doumiya-cp.com/jiin.php?eid=00127|website=Dōmiya Carving Photo Museum|access-date=2019-08-10}}. The '''main hall, offering hall, and worship hall''' were designated '''Nagareyama City Cultural Properties in 1980'''{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine (Main Hall, Offering Hall, Worship Hall)|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Nagareyama City Official Website|access-date=2019-08-10}}. === Sacred Spring === A passage in '''"Kogane Kiko"''' (小金紀行) written in '''late Edo period''' describes the shrine’s '''sacred water spring''', which flows from a hollow in an ancient cedar tree. It has been revered since ancient times as a divine water source{{Cite web|title=Sacred Water|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/lst16229/dt3521.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}. === Main Festivals === * '''New Year’s Festival''' (January 1) * '''Genshi-sai''' (January 3) * '''Spring Festival''' (April 23) * '''Summer Festival (Oryo-sai)''' (July 14) * '''Grand Festival''' (August 22-23) * '''Shichi-Go-San Festival''' (November 15) * '''Autumn Festival''' (November 23) === Access === * '''By Train''': ** '''5-minute walk''' from [[Toyoshiki Station]] ([[Tobu Urban Park Line|Tobu Noda Line]]) ** '''15-minute walk''' from [[Nagareyama-Ōtakanomori Station]] ([[Tsukuba Express]], Tobu Noda Line) * '''By Car''': Accessible via local roads from '''Nagareyama City''' == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.suwano-mori.com/ Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Nagareyama, Chiba]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qq]][[ja:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (流山市)]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (流山市)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine Nagareyama}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (諏訪神社 (流山市))}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine (流山市)}}

{{Shrine | Name = Komaki Suwa Shrine | image = [[File:Suwa shrine,nagareyama-city,japan.jpg|250px|Suwa Shrine Worship Hall]]

[[File:駒木諏訪神社 - Komaki Suwa Shrine (01).jpg|thumb|left|Sacred Horse Statue]] Later, the '''Heian period''' warlord '''Minamoto no Yoshiie''' (Hachiman Tarō) stopped at the shrine when traveling to [[Mutsu Province]] during the [[Later Three-Year War]]. In honor of this history, the shrine features '''two bronze statues''': the '''"Sacred Horse Statue"''' and the '''"Yoshiie Dedication Horse Statue"''', sculpted by '''Kitamura Seibo'''{{Cite web|title=Yoshiie Dedication Horse|url=http://www.suwano-mori.com/sta16234/index.html|website=Komaki Suwa Shrine Official Website|access-date=2019-08-08}}.
